title: Gap Map Matrix
description: A matrix for locating places where technical activity and policy readiness are not moving at the same pace.

cells.label: High
cells.explanation: Technical work and defence experimentation are moving quickly across autonomy, sensing, and decision support.

cells.label: Growing
cells.explanation: The field combines existing engineering practice with new security and resilience pressures.

cells.label: Growing
cells.explanation: Remote sensing, maritime awareness, and autonomous systems are expanding the technical surface of Arctic policy.

cells.label: High
cells.explanation: Advanced chip design and manufacturing remain a concentrated and strategically important technical frontier.

cells.label: Uneven
cells.explanation: Technical, legal, and operational communities are active, but they do not always frame the same risks together.

cells.label: Early
cells.explanation: Protection, ownership, resilience, and security responsibilities remain dispersed across institutions.

cells.label: Uneven
cells.explanation: The policy implications of emerging surveillance capabilities are shared across environmental, security, and Indigenous governance concerns.

cells.label: Developing
cells.explanation: Industrial strategy has begun to catch up through investment, workforce, and supply-chain initiatives.

cells.label: Low
cells.explanation: Public debate often lags behind the technical and operational choices shaping military uses of AI.

cells.label: Low
cells.explanation: The strategic importance of undersea systems is easy to miss until a disruption makes it visible.

cells.label: Low
cells.explanation: Many people still encounter the Arctic as a regional issue rather than a connected security and infrastructure field.

cells.label: Partial
cells.explanation: The strategic importance of chips is clearer than many of the technical and institutional dependencies behind it.
